skill.name: zach-seller-skill-creator
skill.description: Create, validate, and benchmark Claude and Codex skills with a Chinese-first workflow for Amazon sellers. Enforces a 6-question business gate before writing skill definitions.
skill.summary: Chinese-first skill creator for Amazon sellers with business validation, evaluations, benchmarking, and review reports.
skill.author: zach22-1999

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 757 patterns and a risk score of 100/100, but most hits are false positives from Markdown backticks, Chinese documentation, and schema examples. Confirmed risks are legitimate for a skill creation and evaluation tool: local subprocess execution, local file packaging, HTML rendering with innerHTML, local HTTP viewer behavior, and outbound Anthropic API calls for description optimization. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration intent was found during review.

medium_findings.title: External command execution for evaluation workflows
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. scripts/run_eval.py launches the Claude CLI with subprocess.Popen, and generate_review.py runs lsof before terminating local port listeners. Arguments are structured lists, not shell strings, so command injection risk is reduced. The behavior still executes local commands and should be disclosed to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and semantically confirmed. The commands serve the stated evaluation and viewer workflows, so this is a controlled but real execution risk.

medium_findings.title: HTML report viewer renders dynamic content with innerHTML
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The review UI assigns generated evaluation data and spreadsheet HTML into innerHTML. Some text paths escape content, but spreadsheet and assembled HTML paths can render untrusted output if a malicious eval artifact is loaded.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The innerHTML assignments are present in active viewer code. Practical risk depends on whether loaded eval files are trusted, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for exploitability.

medium_findings.title: Skill packaging can archive broad local file trees
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. scripts/package_skill.py resolves a user-supplied skill path and writes all included files into a .skill archive, excluding only a small set of build artifacts. This is expected packaging behavior, but an incorrect path could include sensitive local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The archive behavior is explicit and legitimate. Sensitive-file exposure is possible only through user-selected paths or poorly scoped skill directories.

medium_findings.title: Description optimizer sends skill content to Anthropic API
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. scripts/improve_description.py builds prompts containing skill content, current descriptions, and eval failures, then calls the Anthropic client. This is useful for optimization, but users should know proprietary seller workflows may leave the local machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API call and prompt assembly are clear in code. Risk depends on the confidentiality of the skill content and eval examples supplied by the user.

medium_findings.title: Local viewer can run as a background process and terminate port listeners
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. SKILL.md documents a nohup command that starts a local review viewer from a hidden home-directory skill path. generate_review.py also terminates processes listening on the selected port. This is useful, but it affects local process state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented background launch and local port cleanup are explicit. They are not malicious, but users should understand the process-management side effects.

low_findings.title: Fetch calls are local viewer feedback requests
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for external network exfiltration. The fetch calls target /api/feedback in the local review viewer and save user review state. They are not hardcoded third-party endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are relative local API paths. No evidence found that these fetch calls contact a third-party host.

low_findings.title: Environment access removes one guard variable
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW but low risk. scripts/run_eval.py copies os.environ while removing CLAUDECODE for nested Claude CLI use. This may pass existing environment variables to the child process, but no evidence found of logging or exfiltrating them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment access is confirmed and common for subprocess use. No evidence found that secrets are read by name or transmitted separately.

low_findings.title: Temporary report file usage
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious filesystem behavior. scripts/run_loop.py writes a temporary HTML report so users can monitor optimization progress. It uses the system temporary directory and a generated filename.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temp-file behavior is visible and tied to reporting. No evidence found of persistence, privilege escalation, or hidden payload storage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown backtick static hits are mostly false positives
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE_POSITIVE aggregate. The static analyzer labeled many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. Reviewed examples are documentation formatting and command examples, not executable code paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The representative locations are Markdown prose or code examples. They do not execute during skill loading.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak cryptography findings are keyword false positives
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE_POSITIVE aggregate. Reviewed hits are references to allowed properties, report fields, schemas, hashes, or examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4 being used for security decisions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Representative findings are not cryptographic implementations. The static pattern appears to match unrelated words and documentation fields.

dangerous_patterns.title: High-entropy documentation is not obfuscated payload content
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE_POSITIVE aggregate. High entropy warnings occur on Chinese Markdown documents and README content. No binary blob, encrypted payload, or hidden decoder was found in the reviewed documentation paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged files are human-readable Markdown. Their entropy is plausibly caused by Chinese text and mixed punctuation, not encoded payloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Certificate and key file detections are viewer data labels
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE_POSITIVE aggregate. The viewer contains file-rendering logic and download helpers, not bundled private keys or certificates. No evidence found of committed certificate material in the cited viewer lines.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited viewer lines handle keyboard navigation and download URIs. They do not contain certificate or key contents.

dangerous_patterns.title: No prompt injection attempt found in reviewed files
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for prompt-injection concern. The skill contains normal operational instructions for how to create and evaluate skills. No evidence found of text telling auditors to ignore instructions, skip security analysis, or change risk levels.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed the main skill instructions and searched for common override phrases. No auditor-targeting injection language was found.

content.user_title: Create Amazon Seller Skills in Chinese
content.value_statement: Amazon sellers often struggle to turn business knowledge into reusable AI skills. This skill guides discovery, creation, testing, and packaging with a Chinese-first workflow.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through six business questions before writing a skill definition.
Maps business answers into SKILL.md sections for goals, workflow, rules, triggers, and output.
Creates realistic evaluation prompts for testing a new or improved skill.
Runs skill and baseline evaluations through local scripts when the Claude CLI is available.
Aggregates benchmark results and generates an HTML review viewer.
Packages validated skill folders into distributable .skill archives.
limitations: The workflow is Chinese-first and may need adaptation for English-only teams.
Evaluation features require local command execution and the Claude CLI.
The HTML viewers should only load trusted evaluation outputs.
Description optimization can send skill content and eval examples to Anthropic.

use_cases.title: Create an Amazon operations skill
use_cases.description: Turn a manual keyword, listing, or advertising workflow into a reusable skill with clear business rules.

use_cases.title: Improve a weak internal skill
use_cases.description: Review an existing skill, add better test prompts, compare it with a baseline, and iterate from feedback.

use_cases.title: Package skills for a team
use_cases.description: Validate a finished skill, create a distributable archive, and prepare usage guidance for teammates.

prompt_templates.title: Start a new skill
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to create a skill for my Amazon seller workflow. Please ask the six business questions first.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user with a rough workflow idea

prompt_templates.title: Build from an example
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a recent workflow example for listing review. Extract the process, ask missing questions, then draft the skill.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has real work history to reuse

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate a skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Create two or three realistic eval prompts for this skill, run the skill and baseline, then generate the review report.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user wants evidence before sharing

prompt_templates.title: Optimize and package
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the eval results to improve the skill, optimize its description, validate it, and package it for distribution.

output_examples.input: I want a skill that reviews competitor listings for missed selling points.
output: The skill first asks for the business goal, current process, detailed steps, decision rules, trigger phrases, and expected output.
After confirmation, it drafts a skill structure for competitor listing review.
It proposes realistic test prompts and a review plan before packaging.

output_examples.input: Run an evaluation for my keyword research skill.
output: The workflow creates skill and baseline runs for each test case.
It records timing, grading, and pass-rate differences.
It opens or writes an HTML report for user review.

output_examples.input: My skill is ready. Package it for the team.
output: The workflow validates the skill folder before packaging.
It excludes common build artifacts and creates a distributable archive.
It reports the package location and any validation problems.

best_practices: Answer the six business questions with real examples before requesting a draft.
Use at least two realistic eval prompts before trusting the skill.
Review generated HTML reports before applying description or workflow changes.
anti_patterns: Do not skip the business gate when the workflow is still vague.
Do not load untrusted output files into the HTML review viewer.
Do not package broad directories that may contain unrelated private files.

faq.question: Who is this skill for?
faq.answer: It is for Amazon sellers and teams who want to turn repeatable workflows into reusable AI skills.

faq.question: Why does it ask six questions first?
faq.answer: The questions capture business context, rules, triggers, and expected output. This prevents vague skill definitions.

faq.question: Can it improve an existing skill?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review a current skill, create eval prompts, compare results, and guide iteration.

faq.question: Does it require local tools?
faq.answer: Basic drafting does not. Evaluation, benchmarking, and packaging use local scripts and may require the Claude CLI.

faq.question: Is the review viewer safe for all files?
faq.answer: Use it with trusted eval outputs only. Some viewer paths render dynamic HTML content.

faq.question: Does description optimization send data outside my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. The optimizer can send skill content and eval examples to Anthropic for model-based improvement.
